projects.hotstuff.overview.title: site.hopen
Overview
site.hclose
projects.hotstuff.overview.1: An interest in tooling, casting, and experimental metalwork drove a requirement for a furnace and, potentially,
projects.hotstuff.overview.2: A prototype burner was constructed using available materials, existing tooling, and publicly available designs
developed by various makers — predominantly in the United States — including Ron Reil, Larry Zoeller,
and Richard Mize. Refractory materials were also required. Commercial refractory products can be expensive, so
a home-made refractory cement was formulated using readily available materials.
projects.hotstuff.overview.3: The burner was developed first. It was reasoned that if an efficient and reliable burner could not be produced,
the remainder of the project would be moot. The prototype burner was considered inherently resistant to flashback
because the positive propane pressure prevented air from bleeding back into the gas line. Early testing demonstrated
that the prototype required a significantly larger air intake than originally anticipated, and the design was
modified accordingly. Later burners adopted a revised geometry.
projects.hotstuff.overview.4: The home-made refractory proved surprisingly effective although, as expected, commercial refractory materials
performed better in terms of durability and thermal resilience.
The furnace has been used to melt lead, ZAMAK, aluminium, and copper. Subsequent forge designs were used to produce
small decorative pieces, tools, and experimental workpieces. Only a single prototype furnace was constructed, but
two prototype forges were built before a satisfactory design was settled upon.
projects.burners.keypoints.title: site.hopen
Key Points - Burners
site.hclose
projects.burners.keypoints.1: Burner jets were fabricated from inexpensive, repurposed commercial MIG tips.
projects.burners.keypoints.2: Tubing, in later designs, was silver-soldered using a high-melting-point brazing alloy.
projects.burners.keypoints.3: Construction relied predominantly on common commercial plumbing fittings and readily available materials.
projects.burners.keypoints.4: Limited tooling availability was treated as a hard design constraint throughout development.
projects.burners.keypoints.5: In the absence of a 7/8" drill bit, a bank-of-holes arrangement was adopted for the burner intake.
The rationale was analogous to multi-valve intake arrangements used in high-performance engines and
the distributed inlet geometry used in pulse-jet leaf-valve systems.
projects.furnace.keypoints.title: site.hopen
Key Points - Furnace
site.hclose
projects.furnace.keypoints.1: Prototype refractory formulations were developed from inexpensive, locally available materials before commercial refractory products were introduced.
projects.furnace.keypoints.2: Furnace shell construction prioritised simplicity, serviceability, and thermal isolation.
projects.furnace.keypoints.3: Formwork for the home-made castable refractory was fabricated from salvaged Zincalume sheet.
projects.furnace.keypoints.4: The furnace design proved adequate for melting low- and medium-temperature metals including lead, ZAMAK, aluminium, and copper.
projects.furnace.keypoints.5: Thermal performance, refractory durability, and burner stability were evaluated empirically during operation.
projects.forges.keypoints.title: site.hopen
Key Points - Forges
site.hclose
projects.forges.keypoints.1: Multiple forge geometries were prototyped before a satisfactory design was selected.
projects.forges.keypoints.2: Forge development emphasised ease of construction, thermal efficiency, and burner stability.
projects.forges.keypoints.3: Refractory selection and burner positioning significantly affected heating uniformity and
projects.forges.keypoints.4: Earlier forges used twin burners derived from the furnace burner design. The final forge
employed an entirely new approach; however, cast-iron flares proved unsuitable — they melted.
projects.forges.keypoints.5: The completed forge designs were suitable for small tooling, decorative metalwork, and experimental

projects.hotstuff.discussion.1: An interest in tooling and experimental work drove a requirement for a furnace and, possibly, a forge.
In the early 90s I had acquired two workshop manuals from 'The Workshop Series'. One was focused on
building a charcoal burner from a 40Gal drum and scrap sheet metal — the other was a solid-fuel
furnace that was built in a 12Gal oil drum. Time was, however, a premium and the furnace made use of
